title: Exploring 'The Eye of the Wolf': Reading from a Character's Viewpoint
explanation_chunks: Today, we're diving into an exciting story called 'The Eye of the Wolf'. We'll read an excerpt and try to understand it from the wolf's perspective, which means we're looking at the world through his eyes and thoughts.
When a writer tells a story from a specific character's viewpoint, they help us understand what that character sees, feels, and thinks. This makes the story much more personal and interesting.

As you read, pay close attention to the clues the writer gives you. What does the wolf notice about the boy? What are the wolf's secret thoughts and feelings? This helps you step into the story yourself.

We'll also look at how the writer uses different types of sentences – long ones for descriptions and short ones for dramatic moments or quick thoughts – to make the story come alive and show the wolf's mood.
Thinking about how questions are used in the text can also reveal a lot about a character. When the wolf asks 'What does he want?', it tells us he's curious, even if he pretends not to be worried.
examples: If you were writing about your dog watching you eat dinner, you might say, 'The human held the delicious bone-like object. My tail wagged with hopeful longing.' This shows the dog's viewpoint.
A short, sharp sentence like 'He stopped.' after a long description of someone running can create a sudden, dramatic feeling.
If a character thinks, 'Why am I here?', it shows they are confused or questioning their situation.
key_points: Understanding a story from a character's viewpoint helps us connect with them.
Writers use descriptions and internal thoughts to reveal a character's viewpoint.
Sentence length can create different effects: long for detail, short for drama or emphasis.
Questions in a story can show what a character is thinking or feeling.

vocabulary.definition: The way a story is told, usually from a character's perspective.
A fenced area where an animal is kept.
To move slightly or change position.
Stated very clearly and directly, leaving no doubt.

explain_again: We're reading a story and trying to think like the wolf! What does he see? What does he feel? The writer uses short sentences for excitement and long sentences for details, and even questions to show what the wolf is thinking.
fun_analogy: Reading from a character's viewpoint is like putting on their special glasses – everything looks a bit different, and you see things they notice that you might not have seen otherwise!
